abstract.para: &systemd; provides the TasksMax parameter to limit the number of tasks (processes and threads) within a unit or slice. This serves as a critical defense against fork bombs and runaway processes that could otherwise exhaust the system's Process ID (PID) pool.

topic.para: While upstream &systemd; defaults to 15% of the kernel global limit (viewable via sysctl kernel.pid_max), &productnameshort; overrides this behavior to provide maximum compatibility for high-performance workloads.

section.title: Identifying default TasksMax values
section.para: In &productnameshort;, the default TasksMax value for both system units and user slices is set to infinity. This means no restriction is enforced by default. These overrides are located in the following vendor configuration files:
To verify the effective DefaultTasksMax on your system, run:

important.para: While infinity prevents service disruption due to reaching task limits, it leaves the system vulnerable to resource exhaustion. We recommend setting explicit limits for critical production services.

section.title: Overriding the DefaultTasksMax value
section.para: To set a global default limit for all system units, create a drop-in file at /etc/systemd/system.conf.d/90-system-tasksmax.conf. For example, to limit each unit to 256 tasks:
Apply the configuration and verify the change:

section.title: Setting per-service limits
section.para: Specific applications, such as large databases, often require more tasks than the global default. You can override the limit for a specific service (for example, MariaDB) using systemctl set-property:
This command creates a persistent drop-in file at /etc/systemd/system/mariadb.service.d/50-TasksMax.conf. Verify the new limit in the Tasks: line of the service status:

section.title: Configuring task limits for user sessions
section.para: User sessions often require higher limits than system services. To set a custom default for all users, create /etc/systemd/system/user-.slice.d/40-user-taskmax.conf:

tip.para: If a TasksMax limit is too restrictive, the system logs will report errors such as Failed to fork (Resource temporarily unavailable). Incrementally increase the limit until the workload stabilizes.
